elements.sent-2411-001: Large language models (LLMs) deliver impressive results but face challenges from increasing model sizes and computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-002: Structured pruning reduces model size and speeds up inference but often causes uneven degradation across domains, leading to biased performance.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this, we propose DRPruning, which incorporates distributionally robust optimization to restore balanced performance across domains, along with further improvements to enhance robustness.
elements.sent-2411-004: Experiments in monolingual and multilingual settings show that our method surpasses similarly sized models in pruning and continued pretraining over perplexity, downstream tasks, and instruction tuning.
elements.sent-2411-005: We further provide analysis demonstrating the robustness of our method towards various domains and distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, our method automatically determines optimal reference losses and data ratios, suggesting potential for broader applications.
elements.sent-2411-008: Large language models (LLMs) have advanced rapidly, achieving impressive results across a wide range of tasks~\citep{MultitaskMultilingualMultimodal_2023, ChatGPTGoodTranslator_2023, MathematicalCapabilitiesChatGPT_2023, ChatGPTKnowledgeableInexperienced_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, this progress has come with increasing model sizes, significantly raising computational costs for both training and inference, which impacts their accessibility.
elements.sent-2411-010: Structured pruning is a promising approach to reduce model size~\citep{DeepCompression_HMD16, LearningStructured_WWW+16 , but it often causes uneven performance degradation across domains, leading to biased capabilities and unfair downstream task performance~\citep{ShearedLLaMA_XGZC23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: To address this, we propose a distributionally robust pruning method that dynamically adjusts the data distribution during training to ensure balanced performance across domains.
elements.sent-2411-012: Building on distributionally robust optimization (DRO; \citealp{DistributionallyRobust_OSHL19, DistributionallyRobust_SKHL20 ), which aims to optimize worst-case performance across distributions, a reference loss is defined for each domain as a target.}
elements.sent-2411-013: A higher weight is then assigned to domains with larger deviations from this reference loss, while not straying too far from a predefined reference data ratio.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, setting these hyperparameters is challenging, and suboptimal configurations often result in poor outcomes~\citep{DistributionallyRobust_ZLL+21 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: To resolve this, we introduce a method that dynamically adjusts hyperparameters during training.
elements.sent-2411-016: Domains with worse performance often exhibit higher losses, deviating further from the expected loss after sufficient training.
elements.sent-2411-017: Using scaling laws~\citep{ScalingLaws_KMH+20, ScalingLaws_GFF+21 , we predict the loss after training as the reference, where larger deviations indicate poorer performance, thereby promoting capability recovery in these areas.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, we gradually increase the reference data ratio for domains with greater deviations, ensuring robustness across a wider range of distributions, particularly more challenging ones.
elements.sent-2411-019: We validate our approach through experiments in monolingual and multilingual settings, which represent varying degrees of distributional shift.
elements.sent-2411-020: Our model consistently outperforms \citet{ShearedLLaMA_XGZC23 and other LLMs in both pruning and continued pretraining, as measured by perplexity (-5.59\% and -5.60\%, respectively), downstream tasks (+1.27\% and +1.78\%, respectively), and instruction tuning (55.4\% win rate).}
elements.sent-2411-021: Particularly in multilingual settings, our model achieves +2.95\% in downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-022: To further assess domain-specific performance, we develop a sentence continuation benchmark using existing unlabeled data.
elements.sent-2411-023: Results show strong consistency with downstream task performance, confirming the benchmark’s reliability and further demonstrating our improved domain-level capabilities (+17.9\% on average).
elements.sent-2411-024: Moreover, our approach to determining reference losses and data ratios could potentially enhance data efficiency across a broader range of applications, driving further advancements in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-026: We tackle domain imbalance in structured pruning by introducing a distributionally robust pruning method, that dynamically adjusts data ratios during training to ensure robustness against distributional shifts.
elements.sent-2411-027: We demonstrate the method's effectiveness through extensive experiments in monolingual and multilingual settings.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our method surpasses existing ones in perplexity, downstream tasks, instruction tuning, and domain-specific evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-029: We offer refined reference losses and data ratios for training, which can be applied more broadly to enhance various model training processes and contribute to advancements for LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-030: To prune the model to any target configuration, we adopt structured pruning based on Sheared Llama \citep{ShearedLLaMA_XGZC23 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: For each granularity $i$, we learn pruning masks $Z = \{\mathbf{z ^i \mid \mathbf{z ^i \in \mathbb{R ^{D_i \}$ that determine whether substructures are pruned or retained, where $z^i_j = 0$ indicates pruning of the $j$-th substructure.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: We apply pruning across granularities that include transformer layers, hidden dimensions, attention heads, and FFN intermediate dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-033: To parameterize the masks, we use the $\ell_0$ regularization method~\citep{LearningSparse_LWK18 with hard concrete distributions that concentrate probability mass at 0 or 1.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We further use Lagrange multipliers to ensure the pruned model meets the target configuration.
elements.sent-2411-035: Specifically, if exactly \( t^i \) parameters must be retained for \( \mathbf{z ^i \), we impose the constraint: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \tilde{\ell}^i = \lambda^i \left(\sum_j z^i_j - t^i \right) + \phi^i \left(\sum_j z^i_j - t^i \right)^2. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The final training loss integrates these constraints with the language modeling loss of the pruned model, jointly optimizing the model parameters \(\theta\) and pruning masks \(\mathbf{z}\), where \(\mathbf{z}\) typically uses a higher learning rate.}
elements.sent-2411-036: After pruning, we retain the highest-scoring components.
elements.sent-2411-037: To mitigate uneven domain performance after pruning, we apply distributionally robust optimization (DRO; \citealp{DistributionallyRobust_OSHL19, DistributionallyRobust_SKHL20 ) to improve the model's robustness to distribution shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-038: DRO seeks a model $\theta$ that performs well across a set of potential test distributions $\mathcal{Q}$ over $n$ domains.
elements.sent-2411-039: Formally, we aim to: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \underset{\theta}{\operatorname{minimize}} \sup _{Q \in \mathcal{Q}} \mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \sim Q}[\ell(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} ; \theta)]. \end{aligned} \label{eq:minmax}\end{equation} To solve the min-max optimization, we use the iterative best response (IBR) algorithm \citep{fudenberg1998theory .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Each iteration consists of first performing Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) on the current data distribution $\mathbf{q ^t$, followed by updating the data distribution using worst-case weights based on the current parameters.. Formally, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \theta^{t+1} \leftarrow \underset{\theta}{\operatorname{argmin}} \sum_i q_i^t \ell\left(\theta ; D_i\right), \mathbf{q}^{t+1} \leftarrow \underset{\mathbf{q} = \{q_1, \ldots, q_n\} \in \mathcal{Q}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \sum_i q_i \ell\left(\theta^{t+1} ; D_i\right). \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} \theta^{t+1} \leftarrow \underset{\theta}{\operatorname{argmin}} \sum_i q_i^t \ell\left(\theta ; D_i\right), q^{t+1} \leftarrow \underset{q = \{q_1, \ldots, q_n\} \in Q}{\operatorname{argmax}} \sum_i q_i \ell\left(\theta^{t+1} ; D_i\right). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-041: In Section \ref{sec:DRPruning , we introduce our overall procedure to utilize these components to design an effective pruning method, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:main .}}

elements.sent-2411-141: Finally, ``LLMs Verification'' scores both options and filters out cases where the scores don’t match the true answers.

elements.paragraph-2411-020: Continued pretraining from the pruned model underperforms from the pre-trained ones.
elements.sent-2411-164: Continued pretraining from the pruned model result in a slight performance drop (average -0.48), despite the increased parameters (1.8B vs. 1.5B).
elements.sent-2411-165: This contrasts with \citet{ShearedLLaMA_XGZC23 , where continued pretraining on a smaller model shows minimal gains.}
elements.sent-2411-166: In our case, using different data and a stronger small model improves performance during continued pretraining, leading to different results.
elements.sent-2411-167: Unstructured pruning \citep{LotteryTicket_FC19, SparseGPTMassive_FA23, SimpleEffective_SLBK23 removes individual weights but offers limited speedup.}
elements.sent-2411-168: This study focuses on structured pruning \citep{DeepCompression_HMD16, LearningStructured_WWW+16 , which removes entire structural components, such as neurons or layers, making it more effective for simplifying models and improving efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-169: In task-specific models, extensive pruning can retain performance \citep{LearningEfficient_LLS+17, PickingWinning_WZG20, BlockPruning_LCSR21, StructuredPruning_XZC22, ZipLMInferenceAware_KFA23 .}
elements.sent-2411-170: However, for large language models (LLMs), as the ratio of training data to model size increases \citep{TrainingComputeOptimal_HBM+22, LlamaOpen_TLI+23, LlamaOpen_TMS+23 , fewer redundant parameters remain, leading to significant performance degradation after pruning.}
elements.sent-2411-171: To counter this, performance recovery techniques like continued pretraining are essential \citep{LLMPrunerStructural_MFW23, LoRAPruneStructured_ZCS+24 .}
elements.sent-2411-172: However, continued pretraining of pruned models reduces loss at different rates across domains compared to training from scratch, resulting in less efficient data utilization \citep{ShearedLLaMA_XGZC23 .}
elements.sent-2411-173: To address this, we employ a distributionally robust pruning method that dynamically adjusts the data distribution during training, ensuring balanced performance across domains.
elements.sent-2411-174: Overparameterized neural networks often perform well on i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-175: To address this, unlike empirical risk minimization (ERM), which minimizes expected loss for a fixed distribution, MultiDDS \citep{BalancingTraining_WTN20 uses a gradient-based meta-learning method to optimize the sampling distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-176: However, MultiDDS increases computational and memory costs.
elements.sent-2411-177: In contrast, DRO \citep{DistributionallyRobust_DY10, RobustSolutions_BHW+12, DataDrivenRobust_BGK14 improves performance without additional complexity \citep{FairnessDemographics_HSNL18, DistributionallyRobust_OSHL19, LargeScaleMethods_LCDS20 .}}
elements.sent-2411-178: DRO finds a model that performs well across multiple possible test distributions.
elements.sent-2411-179: Group DRO \citep{DistributionallyRobust_SKHL20 minimizes the worst-case loss over all domains without constraining potential distribution, while CVaR-Group DRO \citep{DistributionallyRobust_OSHL19 averages the largest $N$ group losses.}}
elements.sent-2411-180: These methods can be overly conservative, as they account for robustness to arbitrary subpopulations.
elements.sent-2411-181: DRO shows strong performance but relies on two main hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-182: The first is the reference loss, usually set by training an additional baseline model \citep{DistributionallyRobust_ZLL+21, TencentsMultilingual_JTL+22 , though this is expensive for LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-183: The second hyperparameter is the reference data ratio, often determined through temperature-based sampling \citep{MassivelyMultilingual_ABF+19, UnsupervisedCrosslingual_CKG+20 or manually \citep{LlamaOpen_TLI+23, Nemotron415B_PPJ+24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-184: However, fixed ratios can hinder model convergence in challenging distributions.
elements.sent-2411-185: We propose a dynamic method that shifts weight toward higher-loss domains, enhancing distribution robustness and improving downstream performance.
elements.sent-2411-186: We present DRPruning, a distributionally robust pruning method that addresses uneven performance degradation across domains during structured pruning.
elements.sent-2411-187: By utilizing and further improving distributionally robust optimization (DRO), our method focuses more on domains with poorer performance, accelerating performance recovery.
elements.sent-2411-188: It outperforms existing models and training methods in both monolingual and multilingual settings, achieving lower perplexity, higher task accuracy, and better instruction tuning.
elements.sent-2411-189: Further analysis demonstrates the robustness of our method against various domains and distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-190: Additionally, the dynamic adjustment of reference loss and data ratios exhibits broad applicability, with strong potential to support balanced training across diverse tasks.
elements.sent-2411-191: Due to computational constraints, we are unable to explore pruning to larger models, i.e., employing smaller pruning ratios.
elements.sent-2411-192: Retaining a larger proportion of the model's parameters may lead to different outcomes in some experiments.
elements.sent-2411-193: For example, it remains to be investigated whether pruning larger models provides benefits, and whether it is better to continue pretraining from a pruned model or from a smaller, fully pre-trained model.
elements.sent-2411-194: Although good performance can be achieved with tens of billions of training samples, this study does not investigate whether training the models to full convergence using hundreds or thousands of billions of samples would yield better results than continuing pretraining from existing pre-trained models under similar settings.
elements.sent-2411-195: We have validated our method's effectiveness in the pruning phase, the pruning recovery phase, and the continued pretraining phase.
elements.sent-2411-196: However, our method is expected to be applicable in broader contexts, such as pretraining from scratch and cross-domain instruction tuning.
elements.sent-2411-197: Broader validation would further demonstrate the superiority of our approach.

elements.sent-2411-233: To perform a detailed analysis of the masks, we extract the masks generated during training and prune the model by removing components with the lowest scores, shaping the model according to the target specifications.
elements.sent-2411-234: We then calculate the probability of the model making consistent pruning decisions for each substructure and examine how different training steps or strategies influence the masks.
elements.sent-2411-235: Specifically, we apply Sheared Llama, constant, and our proposed strategies, using two distinct random seeds for pruning, which yields six unique 1.3B models.
elements.sent-2411-236: We then analyze the similarities across these models.
elements.paragraph-2411-031: The masks converge quickly during training.
elements.sent-2411-237: The convergence speed of the masks during training is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:mask_converge .}
elements.sent-2411-238: Pruning achieves over 75\% similarity within 400 steps and over 95\% within 800 steps, indicating that effective results can be obtained with relatively few pruning steps.
